meta.note: Libya sweep. Thin title-specific record. Four retraceable cases; one further lead (an untitled parliamentary exposé by MP Abdelfattah Chelui, banned 2015) was excluded for having no stated title. Author/era-level leads (al-Koni, al-Neihoum, al-Faqih, Hisham Matar's other works) excluded for lack of a title-specific ban source. The Green Book burnings excluded (opposition destruction of regime propaganda, not a government ban).

entries.title: Chewing Gum
entries.description_ban: Bushnaf's allegorical novel of Libyan life under dictatorship (al-ʿIlka) was banned in Libya the year it appeared under Gaddafi and reportedly remains prohibited. Bushnaf had earlier been jailed twelve years over his play 'When Rats Govern'.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Political ban of a dictatorship allegory; documented by Index on Censorship and the publisher, corroborated in academic literature.

entries.title: A Sun Shining on Shuttered Windows
entries.description_ban: Anthology of 24 Libyan writers under 30 (the 'Yellow Book', edited via Khaled Mattawa / Arete Foundation); banned in Libya amid an obscenity/morality backlash, after which contributors were harassed and several fled or went into hiding.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Morality-driven ban of a young-writers anthology; documented by The Markaz Review.

entries.title: The Spy
entries.description_ban: The Arabic edition was among a truckload of books seized in January 2017 by Al-Marj police in eastern Libya, en route to Benghazi, as allegedly un-Islamic. Coelho publicly protested the seizure of his books.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Transit seizure of a title on religious grounds; reported by France 24 Observers.

entries.title: Bread on Uncle Milad's Table
entries.description_ban: Alnaas's IPAF-winning novel on gender and masculinity. The author states there was no official ban order; after the prize, local militias pressured bookstores not to stock it — de facto retail suppression over its 'sensitive' gender theme.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Militia-driven retail suppression (no formal order); documented in the author's ArabLit interview (medium confidence).
